title: HTTP Clients in TypeScript 7: Fetch to Production SDKs
description: Build typed browser and Node HTTP clients with Fetch, runtime validation, cancellation, CORS, caching, security, deterministic tests, and accessible application evidence. This original, research-backed path uses repeated retrieval, cumulative artifacts, changed-case labs, stakeholder projects, and a performance exam.
outcomes: Demonstrate http semantics through rfc 9110, caching, http/1.1, http/2, http/3, uri resolution, problem details, cookies, https, redirects, credentials, streaming, conditional requests, retries, rate limits, pagination, and observability through an independently verified stakeholder artifact.

Demonstrate typescript 7 standards-based fetch request construction, response handling, runtime validation, cancellation, testing seams, resource ownership, and production transfer evidence through an independently verified stakeholder artifact.

Demonstrate cumulative projects spanning protocol reasoning, safe destination policy, bounded resources, fault injection, accessibility, privacy, performance, release gates, and production defense through an independently verified stakeholder artifact.

competencies.statement: Distinguish client, server, user agent, origin, intermediary, gateway, proxy, and cache roles per exchange. Demonstrate the result through TypeScript 7 strict types plus WHATWG Fetch Request, Response, Headers, URL, streams, and AbortSignal contracts.
misconceptions: A program has one permanent client-or-server identity. This remains false for TypeScript 7 standards-based Fetch.
masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Distinguish client, server, user agent, origin, intermediary, gateway, proxy, and cache roles per exchange. Demonstrate the result through TypeScript 7 strict types plus WHATWG Fetch Request, Response, Headers, URL, streams, and AbortSignal contracts.

misconceptions: An HTTP response transfers the resource itself rather than a representation. This remains false for TypeScript 7 standards-based Fetch.
masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Separate a resource from the selected representation and its metadata, content, and current validity. Demonstrate the result through TypeScript 7 strict types plus WHATWG Fetch Request, Response, Headers, URL, streams, and AbortSignal contracts.
